goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

morning_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena. You're starting today with a solid 77/100 — that's a good score, but I see why it might feel lower than your body's signals suggest.

**Last night was excellent:** you slept 6.5 hours with 92% efficiency and fell asleep remarkably early. Your HRV hit 116, which is a strong sign your nervous system recovered deeply. Physically, you're in a great place.

**The real story is in how you feel.** Yesterday, you reported high energy (86) but very low mood (16), focus (36), and social connection (12) — you only felt "Bored." That's a sharp disconnect from your strong recovery. It's like your body is ready to go, but your mind isn't finding the spark. This often happens when activity declines (your steps are trending down) and sedentary time creeps up (747 minutes yesterday), especially paired with low social interaction.

Nutrition yesterday also had a gap: you ate enough calories, but protein was low at 68g. When you're active and recovering well, protein helps sustain that energy and repair. Today's about bridging these gaps — connecting your physical readiness with how you actually experience the day.

Your actions today focus on maintaining that fantastic sleep rhythm while intentionally adding protein and movement breaks to support your energy and mood.

You've built a strong recovery foundation this week — now let's make sure your day feels as good as your sleep data looks.

assumptions: User can control meal timing and composition
User can take short breaks during work
User has access to protein-rich foods
User can set reminders and have non-screen activities available
soft_constraints: caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits
consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend
leverage high energy/focus for sleep routine implementation

generated_actions.title: When you finish dinner, start a 15-minute non-screen wind-down
generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep onset was remarkably early (69 minutes vs baseline 480), continuing a strong improving trend (-136.99 slope). Yesterday's caffeine timing was well-managed (none after 2pm). Habit-stacking a wind-down to your dinner routine leverages today's 'high energy/focus for sleep routine implementation' soft constraint to lock in this excellent pattern and support your sleep goal.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you start a non-screen wind-down after dinner tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports starting a 15-minute non-screen activity after their last meal

assumptions: You have access to non-screen activities
generated_actions.description: Right after you finish your last meal tonight, put your phone away and spend 15 minutes on a non-screen activity (reading, light stretching, tidying).

generated_actions.title: Add a protein source to your salad lunch
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's nutrition had a protein deficit (68.1g vs baseline 84.25) and is trending downward (-5.38 slope). Your lunch included a 'salad' and a 'cheese croissant' – adding a protein source to the salad directly addresses the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag and the 'caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits' soft constraint.

evaluation.success_definition: Daily protein intake reaches at least 80g (above yesterday's 68.1g)

assumptions: You have access to protein-rich foods
generated_actions.description: When you eat your salad lunch today, add a serving of a high-protein food like grilled chicken, chickpeas, or hard-boiled eggs.

generated_actions.title: After 90 minutes of continuous work, take a 5-minute walk
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's sedentary minutes were elevated (747, above baseline 681.88) and trending upward (+21.71 slope), while steps are trending downward (-1290 slope). This action directly addresses the soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' and fits within your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' hard constraints.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take a 5-minute walk break after prolonged work today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports taking at least one movement break after 90+ minutes of continuous work

assumptions: You can take short breaks during work
generated_actions.description: When you've been working at your desk for about 90 minutes, stand up and take a 5-minute walk around your space or outside.

generated_actions.title: Write one positive sleep intention after brushing teeth
generated_actions.rationale: There's a cross-domain dissociation: excellent objective sleep (91.78% efficiency) contrasts with low subjective wellbeing (mood 16, focus 36). Habit-stacking a brief positive reflection to your teeth-brushing routine can help bridge this perception gap and reinforce your sleep identity, supporting your sleep goal and 'sleep_consistency_recovery' priority.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you write a positive sleep intention after brushing teeth?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the written reflection

assumptions: You have pen and paper available
generated_actions.description: Right after you brush your teeth before bed, write one sentence on paper about what you hope for tonight's sleep (e.g., 'I hope to sleep deeply').

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Reinforce your early bedtime: set a 9:00 PM wind-down reminder
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was objectively excellent with a remarkably early onset (45 minutes after midnight) and long duration (7.03h), continuing a strong improving trend (sleep_onset slope -190.66). Yesterday's caffeine was well-managed (none after 2pm). To lock in this positive pattern and 'leverage high energy/focus for sleep routine implementation' (soft constraint), a structured wind-down will help maintain this early, high-quality sleep.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you start your screen-free wind-down at 9:00 PM tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports starting a non-screen wind-down routine at or close to 9:00 PM

assumptions: You have an alarm or reminder system
You have non-screen activities available
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Set an alarm for 9:00 PM. When it goes off, put your phone away and spend 15 minutes on a non-screen activity (reading, light stretching, tidying) before you plan to sleep.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: When you have your first meal, add a protein-rich side
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's nutrition showed a calorie deficit (1586 kcal vs baseline 1737) and a downward trend (-50.53 slope), alongside the 'nutrition_gap' risk flag. Protein was adequate (93.4g) but adding a protein-rich side early in the day is a simple habit stack that addresses the calorie deficit and supports the soft constraint 'caution with high energy expenditure given recent calorie/protein deficits'.

evaluation.success_definition: Total calorie intake today reaches at least 1700 kcal (above yesterday's 1586 and closer to baseline 1737)

assumptions: You have access to protein-rich foods
previous_day_generated_actions.description: At your first meal of the day (likely breakfast or lunch), include an extra serving of a high-protein food like Greek yogurt, a boiled egg, or a handful of nuts alongside your main dish.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: After 60 minutes of continuous work, take a 3-minute standing stretch break
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's activity was substantial (14k steps) but steps are trending downward (-576.51 slope) and sedentary minutes, while below baseline, are also trending down (-15.06 slope). This action directly addresses the soft constraint 'consider gentle activity to counter sedentary trend' with a tiny, time-bound habit that fits within your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' hard constraints.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take at least one 3-minute standing stretch break after prolonged work today?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports taking at least one movement break after 60+ minutes of continuous work

assumptions: You can take short breaks during work
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you've been working at your desk for about 60 minutes, stand up and stretch your arms overhead, twist your torso gently, and roll your shoulders for 3 minutes before resuming.

must_avoid: remaining seated for the entire break

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Write one line in a sleep journal right after your evening wind-down
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: There is a noted 'subjective_vs_objective_mismatch': yesterday's subjective sleep quality was 75 while objective sleep efficiency was excellent at 92.34%. Habit-stacking a brief reflection to your wind-down routine can help bridge this perception gap, improve subjective sleep experience, and support your sleep goal domain and 'sleep_consistency_recovery' priority.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you write one line in your sleep journal after your wind-down?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing the written reflection after their wind-down routine

assumptions: You have pen and paper available
previous_day_generated_actions.description: Immediately after you finish your 9:00 PM wind-down activity, take 1 minute to write one sentence in a notebook about how you're feeling or your intention for sleep tonight.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena! You said you're feeling an 84 this morning with high energy — that's a solid start.

Let's look at last night first: you got **7 hours of sleep** with 92% efficiency — that's excellent and above your recent average. What's really interesting is your sleep onset was just 45 minutes after midnight, which is *extremely* early for you. Your body recovered beautifully from yesterday — your HRV hit 111, which is way up from your baseline, and your resting heart rate is down to 64. That's a powerful recovery signal. Yesterday, your subjective scores were all maxed out at 100 with stress at just 9, which perfectly aligns with this strong physical recovery. One thing I notice: despite that early bedtime, your last meal was quite late (around 10:30 PM), and that late-eating trend has been accelerating. It doesn't seem to have hurt your sleep last night, but it's worth watching.

Today's about protecting this momentum. Your actions are designed to reinforce that early sleep pattern, gently address a small calorie gap from yesterday, and keep you moving during work hours.

You've built a really strong foundation this week — today's focus is on keeping that positive alignment between how you feel and how your body's performing.

meal_descriptions.description: 5 dates with peanut butter and a small banana
